author: @RH4992
text:
"Humid cold" does NOT exist. High amounts of water vapour only exist in warmer air temperatures. Always look at the "Dew point", not the "relative humidity" to find out how humid it is.
Humidity makes YOU feel warmer because it prevents the body from losing as much heat.

"Relative humidity" is how close the dew point is to the air temperature. The dew point can NEVER be higher than the air temperature.
A dew point of 50°F (10°C) means that the air is dry (even if the RH is 100%).

The WIND SPEED is what makes you feel colder. If there's no wind, it doesn't feel as cold.

author: @richardmouri4556
text: Where are the mechanical engineers? Wet and dry cold, like wet and dry heat, is very different but absolutely dependent upon the relative humidity (look up Psychrometric chart). In cold temperatures, the ideal RH is 50%. Those water molecules trap the heat in the air and you feel warmer. Lower RH and there's not enough water molecules to absorb the heat (from the air or your body) and you feel much colder. In the desert, you can cool your body with a simple water mister. In humid climates all that heat is trapped next to your skin and a mister only makes you feel clammier - unless you put ice in it which will then draw the heat from your body.

author: @Christopher-vj2ie
text:
Cold is cold. Absolutely 100% agree there. But you'll have different effects from dry cold vs wet cold. As a swedish person I have a bit of experience with this since I can experience both at different times of the year. From wet cold in fall/autumn to more dry colds from December to January. 
Wet cold will chew through your insulation, unless you have a water proof layer underneath. You will start to shiver a lot sooner and hypothermia will also kick in faster. Dry cold on the other hand is what will give you frostbite. So if you're not properly covered up, you'll see a lot more of that. 
But at the end of the day, cold is still cold.

author: @deejayimm
text: Dry and cold is far more survivable than wet and cold.

author: @Roland14d
text:
The CA experiments are nice but only a partial test.  There needs to be experiments with people who are not in peak physical condition.  No should loss of body heat be the only criterion.  For instance, every arthritic I know, regardless of age, suffers more from cold-wet.  At 60yoa, cold-wet affects me far more than cold-dry, especially around my old injuries.   

Maybe they can't find enough of us oldies to volunteer for these test?

author: @cuernimus
text: I don't agree with this conclusion.  It doesn't take into account the amount of energy the body needed to produce to maintain that core temperature, and also the temperature of the hands and feet would be helpful (core temperature vs frostbite).  If the wet cold group needed to shiver % more than the dry cold group, then the wet cold would actually be colder, even if the chances of either group experiencing lethal hypothermia would be more or less the same.

author: @refluent
text:
Thinking about it- heat travels to cold.  It seems likely the transfer is more efficient via same elements.  We are made of water to a large degree, so water in the body/ leaks heat to water moisture in clothing/ leaks water moisture to the humidity in the air.  
Regarding the elements;- H2O is not H2O- noone counted the- it is a ratio of volume- not a quantum photo showing the difference in elemental size between Hydrogen and Oxygen.  Hydrogen is actually larger than Oxygen!!!!  
The real question is- In wet cold vs dry cold, are the H elements more or less active vs the O elements and which way around is it if so?

author: @FasterLower
text:
Surely wet air has a greater density than dry air and thus the greater mass. So it would seem logical that a room full of wat air takes more energy to raise to the same temperature than the same room full of dry air.
Also, are dehumidifiers not more efficient than heaters? Electric heaters are of course 100% efficient - all the electricy which goes in comes out as heat. However, are dehuimidifiers not better than this? All the electricity comes out as heat but there is additional energy due to the latent heat of evaporation release when the water vapor condences to liquid. Soo additional energy is released and thus > 100 % efficient. Or am I missing something?

author: @DavidPaulNewtonScott
text: You are right on a hobby horse of mine and as a person who has live in a tent under both conditions i know it is warmer if the air is dry even at night disregarding the sun. Wet air has a larger thermal capacity and so is better at removing heat from the body. I have not checked but I will wager that damp air also has a much higher thermal conductivity. On the flip side in the Philippines my other life the temperature only goes up to 30C but it is unbearably hot and in Portugal my other life the temperature goes up to 40C and its not unbearably hot in fact I like it. The difference is simple the powerfully cooling sweat mechanism doesn't work in the humid Philippines. It's OK in the daytime but once the sun goes down it really jacks up the relative humidity and when a European lies down in bed it is even worse with our unfavourable surface are a to volume ratio. For a long time we have said in the Philippines "the Philippines is hot"

author: @simongross3122
text:
Opposite story in Australia.  Is dry heat different to humid heat?  Well yes - we feel hotter when our sweat can't evaporate and impart that cooling effect to our skin.

But I think that we *feel* cold and heat mostly through our skin, regardless of our core body temperature which our warm-blooded bodies carefully regulates for us.  So I'm not surprised that sticking thermometers up nether regions would fail to find a difference.

author: @davidrunnalls3039
text: An aside to this and may help, In the Highlands of Scotland deer will survive extremely low temperatures - 20 or so. If you have a a wet winter with temperatures at around freezing they will die in their hundreds. Having lived up there for 18 years and had 3 really cold winters you discover that as the temperature falls below - 6 the air starts to dry out. In January of 2010 we had weeks where the temperature day and night never got above freezing sunny during the day. Walk outside it felt cold but not unbearable until you touched the door handle of the garage and you stuck to it! Needs to be - 10 or below for that.

author: @jezlawrence720
text:
I don't know if dry cold makes you feel warmer so much as wet cold makes you feel colder.

I assume it has something to do with the heat properties of moisture: you aren't colder but you're losing heat faster. Your skin - which is your thermosense organ, amongst other things - *notices* that transfer, like you can tell almost immediately when a radiator switches off and starts cooling.

You are therefore much more conscious of that heat loss, compared to a drier cold.

author: @TheCyberSalvager
text: Wet or dry cold? Dry, definitely! Many years ago I spent a week in Switzerland - through one of my previous jobs - and although there was snow everywhere, it seemed a lot more bearable as it was dry. Here in Blightly it's often damp and a lot more for me to deal with in that way. It's like what I often say about weather: Some countries have hot weather, others have cold weather, but Britain has "Can't really be arsed" weather!

I used to live on Baffin Island and grew up in the maritimes and there's no doubt a dry cold is significantly sneakier than a wet cold so -20⁰C is less perceptible than  wet cold at -2⁰C.
First time I figured that out a friend said I was slurring my words and didn't realize I was going hypothermic because it didn't "feel cold". In fact, -20⁰C felt like tee shirt weather in what is essentially a dessert by humidity standards. 
Love the content 🤗

author: @wrekced
text: @Robert, I think the difference that humidity makes has to do with the thermal mass of water versus the thermal mass of dry air. If there is more water in the air, it takes more energy to raise the temperature of the air. Also, water transfers energy better than air. So more humidity increases the thermal conductivity of the air around you. That means you cool of slightly faster in humid air... I think the Canadian tests are measuring the wrong thing. They should measure how the speed at which an unprotected human cools off changes as the humidity increases. They should also compare how much energy it takes to warm up humid air versus dry air.

author: @WilliamTythas
text:
I wonder if it has to do with the thermal conductivity of Air VS water (high Humidity) - air is a thermal insulator and doesn't conduct the heat from our skin (temp "travels" from hot to cold only) as easily/readily as with a higher moisture content Air - it may not change our core temp but the sensation of hot and cold is much more sensitive.  

I would also think that test of the military to be kinda flawed in the desired research because they used subjects under constant exercise (creating excess personal heat)... try this same technique just sitting in a trench or foxhole. I would think the results would be different.  I would just think there is also the evaporative effect also - misting fans feel a lot cooler than just a fan in the blowing the same temp air (water pulls the heat from the skin)

American Joke:

3 men were talking:

1st man:  Where are you guys from?

2nd man:   I'm from Phoenix Arizona.

1st man:  It gets awfully hot out there doesn't it?

2nd man:  Yeah,  but it's a dry heat,  it doesn't bother you as much,  Say, where are you from?

1st man:  I'm from Minneapolis,  Minnesota.

2nd man:  It gets awfully cold up there,  doesn't it?

1st man:  Yes,  but it's a dry cold.  It doesn't bother you as much.

2nd man to 3rd man:  Well,  you might as well tell us where you're from,  too.

3rd man:  I'm from Miami,  Florida.

2nd man:  It rains a lot in Florida,  doesn't it?

3rd man:  Yeah,  but it's a dry rain.  It doesn't bother you as much.

author: @vidard9863
text:
wet cold is worse. the canadian studies failed to measure what happens to people who stop moving, and total caloric use.
in addition to the decrease in r value of the clothing the human body generates heat. the moisture evaporates near the skin taking warmth, and cools away from the skin, effectively causing you to have a larger surface area and leas r value. to maintain body heat your must generate enough energy to overcome these one or two phase changes. that isn't a problem when you are exerting yourself, but when you sit down or sleep it can be fatal.
tldr, if you are exerting yourself enough nothing is really too cold, it is only when you stop that the wet cold really becomes a problem.

author: @deathstarHQ
text: Cold is Cold at the end of the day.... but what about comparing heavy downpours rain, and that fine rain that soaks ya through... it's a difficult one, a sudden downpour causes humans to weirdly behave in an almost identical manner, as they instantly enter panic mode, and auto rain reactions take over the entire body, arms trying to find brollies which they didn't bring or even own, frantic surges of pocket hunting, who knows what they are looking for, then they decide to zip their jacket up getting the whole order of stuff to do completely arse about face, but develop some kind of zipping seizure with the right hand not knowing what the left is up to, they move on to heaving the open jacket over their heads and standing in a shop doorway like a drowned rat.  Fine rain, different story... not a peep, couple of hours shopping walking the dog in fine rain and they need to be reminded by every passer by what the weather is like, us brits hey... stop for 10 minutes and have a natter in fine rain, no one gives a sh*t... then eventually get home, and take your jacket off, which now weighs more than you, top of the range 100% waterproof walking boots get kicked off, socks drenched, nothing escaped it's magic act of turning everything it touches into a sponge, so even though you don't notice it, you unknowingly end up taking a gallon of water home.... but there's always a higher chance of a rainbow..... which is nice!.... i was gonna include drizzle, but you can't even say the word without coming across as miserable.  I feel sorry for people who wake up to a day of grey gloomy skies and drizzle in Gimsby. 🤣😂

author: @RG-cf6dn
text: Greetings Robert,  was wondering if you might do some videos on heat exchangers as there are many variants and it'd be interesting.  You do many videos on varied ways of heating but these tend be small indoor or like the Rocket outdoors.  Most folks have either indoor heating towards their homes or cabin or external heating and/or cooling.  I guess practical application one might say and that involves heat exchangers.  A car radiator has a differing design than that of of a coil like heat exchanger for a furnace for example and I guess the old wall air conditioner heat exchanger is different again as are the old steam or forced water cast iron radiators.  How much efficiency these might have, how much lost?  For example I saw a place saying they're 8x8 inch exchanger via forced water and 500 CFM fan push out 18,000 BTUs.

author: @C-M-E
text:
I will 150% agree that humidity with cold feels worse! The first 20 or so years of my life, I grew up in a cold, northern climate where it got bone-chilling cold, however even when it was 0*F, it was dry and you felt like you could get away with a light jacket. 
On the flip side, I've been in the south another 20 odd years, and the humid cold when its 20-40*F you feel like you're freezing to death and there aren't enough layers to do you justice without compensating with physical exertion. I've tried to explain it to people for a long time unsuccessfully, that they say your blood is thicker up north (which physiologically I could say may be true) as I much prefer dealing with heat and feel perfectly fine in long slacks even well into the mid 90s.

So the way they are measuring the temperature may not be telling the whole story.

author: @Jim1alaska

OK, before I watch it, wet cold's worst. minus forty degrees dry, you can put on another layer or so and feel toasty. Been there, done that, often.  

Thirty two F. or zero C. wet and blowing, no matter how much you wrap yourself, hypothermia is just around the corner.

replies.author: @Jim1alaska
replies.text: OK Boss, I watched it all the way through and I still stand by my position on the subject. You touched on it, wet or damp clothing does not insulate as well making it harder to maintain body generated heat.

author: @scotttovey
text:
It's not really about being warmer or being colder.
It's about how comfortable it feels.

It's the same with heating your home.
In the winter time the air is less humid that it otherwise would be during spring and summer months. 
As a result, you feel less warm at the same temperature in the winter time than you do in the summer time.
It's the same air temperature, the difference is how you feel.

I keep my thermostat set at 78f and their are days when I feel like the temperature is less than 60f degrees. That's partly due to a health issue, but make no mistake about it. Put a young healthy individual in my apartment and he or she would be uncomfortably warm. 

Despite these facts, on a humid summer day, that same 60f degrees is quite comfortable and that 78f degrees, becomes very uncomfortable.

author: @bigcheesedog2645
text:
IDK if it is the damp cold or getting wet and then getting really cold wet = dead.  For myself, temps around -5 c are miserable because it is so hard to stay dry. Sunny no wind and -10c is a great day to be outside.  Once you get past -20c and add wind it's a whole new level of misery.  I spent 5 years in Vancouver BC, I am guessing the climate is about the same as in the UK, you tell winter and summer apart by how cold the rain is, but the most miserable thing for this prairie boy was the lack of sun, sorry it just does not hold a candle to a Canadian prairie winter.  Come ice fishing at -35c and then we can talk about damp cold :).

The big advantage of dryer air is it takes way less energy to heat where I live now has these things called Chinooks through the winter when we get a strong steady wind from the west we can warm up dramatically I think the record is -20c to +20c  in one hour the mechanic is  as the air goes over the mountains it losses 2degrees freedom per 1000 feet of elevation gain dumping most of the moisture content on the west facing slopes of the mountains of British Columbia as that air comes back down the mountains it gains 4 degrees freedom .

author: @zipperdeedooha
text: Watched a talk on YT by an Arctic explorer who mentioned that Eskimos NEVER run. Not even in an emergency. Running creates sweat and thereby can evaporate and dissipate body heat. So no running please.

author: @spiritusinfinitus
text: Humid air contains more mass than drier air. This means that a room full of wet air has a greater total mass than a room full of dry air. The room with the greater total mass will surely take longer to heat up.

author: @rabidwasp
text:
Couple of comments: When the air is humid, ones clothing tends to absorb a certain amount of moisture from the air. This moisture then proceeds to slowly evaporate, using a small amount of heat from our bodies to accomplish this. This heat loss would likely not affect our core temperature, but would increase the overall load on our metabolisms, depleting our energy reserves faster.

The other point is that young, fit soldiers are going to have above average metabolic health, so masking the effects of cold - running a similar experiment with middle aged or elderly subjects would be likely to give a different result.

author: @ryanlebeck259
text:
As a bit of a scientist where I weigh in on the subject of cold is to gently remind others that water has the natural ability to transfer heat until it achieves thermal equilibrium.

Yes it is true that wet air will seem more cold than dry air, and ostensibly there is not much difference to core temperature, the moisture in the air will have a minor affect on thermal balancing factors simply because it is water and as a 'material' water has rules that it follows just like glass or metal does.

Dry air doesn't take any more energy to warm than wet air at the end of the day, though it seems as though it takes less energy to KEEP dry air warm.

I used the word SEEMS deliberately because nothing I say is going to make you feel snuggly warm when your shivering in your own home 😉.

Pressure plate from a truck on the hottest part of the stove,  it would still be warm  hours after the fire had been out.

author: @Ludotooki
text:
This makes me think of the differences in energy required to maintain a temperature in a room linked to the humidity of that room. The energy to heat the space would presumably remain the same, but perhaps 'denser' air or moister, at any rate, has a slower rate of ideal temperature decay. In other words, I wonder if moist air makes temperature change slow down enough to have a psychosomatic effect that changes "I feel cold in wet cold" to "I can't get warm in wet cold" to "Wet cold is colder".

If from an air conditioning standpoint, it takes less energy to cool a dryer environment, then I believe perhaps our sensory receptors are in a way 'wired' to seek a less energy intense environment for the purposes of regulating our internal temperature. A kind of hormonal thermopile! 

Always a fun watch, Mr. Murray-Smith. Cheers.

author: @wibblywobblyidiotvision
text:
There is actually a major difference in the sensation of cold between wet and dry cold.  I say this with 20 years of experience as a chairlift operator in the French Alps.  I know cold, nearly lost some fingertips from frostbite working outside at -10 or so.  Dry cold day, ironically enough.  But anyway, the difference is very much to do with humidity, and particularly how it affects the skin.  A humid cold acts the same way that sweating does - it sucks heat away from your skin and you feel cold, colder than the ambient temperature.  And while this may have little to no affect on your core temperature, the difference in the sensation of cold is enormous.  I would rather spend a day at -15C or below, a temperature which makes the hairs in your nose freeze up (it's an odd sensation, you have to experience it to believe it) than one at around zero.  There is some truth in the fact that very cold days around here are often sunny, but even when they aren't there is pretty much zero humidity in the air.  Sure, it's cold.  Bitterly cold, "don't take your gloves off" cold, but the cold doesn't creep through your clothes the same way that it does on a humid, maybe foggy, day at 1C.

And anyway, who'd trust the Canadians?  They'll swear blind that Quebec is habitable.

author: @malHHkenny
text:
Please keep considering that perceived warmth, any given ambient temperature and actual body temperature are three very different things.

As you’ve shown, the relation between perceived warmth and humidity is a wild-card. I’d like to point out that body temperature range can be a very serious matter, given some medical circumstances (one of which may only be advanced age).

This may get verbose. Bear with me.

A body under stress needs proper hydration to survive. That’s easy to do, but to me, this also indicates that scientific analysis can be applied. The problem is in measuring evaporation rate on skin surface. Tricky. But evaporation consideration can be a medical-science necessity. This may seem less than significant, intuitively. Extremes can provide significant data worth looking at.

I have a form of Parkinson’s disease that involves an inability to properly regulate body temperature and a dangerous susceptibility to hypothermia. Perceived warmth can be very dangerous. Fortunately there are digital oral thermometers readily available. Ambient temperature below 65 degrees can feel warm until I start to feel icy and ill. Measured body temp falls toward hypothermia. (The oral thermometer reading falls to the low 90s.) Inattention could be tragic. The remedy, according to my doctors is to wrap up warmly, raise the temperature in the room and — equally important — re-hydrate. There are several ways. One part of re-hydration is to get the room decently humidified.

Point being: A 68 degree Fahrenheit room that’s not dry, is a room that I’m safe in — regardless of whatever perceived warmth. A dry room at 65... Not so much. When it’s a matter of survival, perceived warmth is moot. Actual temperatures are what matters.

text: Core body temperature is one thing: Ok so it makes no difference to any immediate danger of hyperthermia. But I bet there is a genuine measurable difference on the surface of the skin, where our nerve-endings are!

author: @SeanBZA
text: Well, my mother was easy, she never wanted to be as cold as she was, when she was in the Gulag in Siberia. Thus she moved to central Africa, and then to South Africa, where the winter never drops below freezing, at least by me on the coast. Central Africa the only way to tell summer from winter, was winter it did not rain every day from 6 to 9 morning and evening. Enough rain that a drainage ditch was 2m wide by 1m deep, just to handle the street runoff, and a concrete slab bridge over it for your driveway.

author: @totherarf
text:
Hmm ..... not sure that hosing squaddies down with freezing water and checking their temp is going to tell you anything other than you being lucky to still be sucking air in afterwards!

This is especially problamatic if they are all running round! The body generates heat and gets rid of the excess by eather sweat (like being hosed down?) breathing or by convection with a tiny bit of radiating going on too! Whatever you do, as long as you do not enter hypothermia, you will not affect the bodies core temp! 
But that is not really the issue here!

The issue is Is a damp atmosphere at a given temp worse than a dry one at the same temp?
My gut feeling is that damp (not wet) would conduct heat better than dry. It takes energy to cause the molecules of water droplets to vaporise and move away from the body! .... but I could be wrong! ;o)

author: @keithianlocke
text:
Well Robert, I grew up in the Norfolk with the broads, then lived in the Hghlands of Scotland for 9yrs and worked outside all year round. Now I live back in the cambridgeshire fens.
Wet cold feels much colder than Dry cold. The Highlands have dry air, and we were able to work in the cairngorms erecting fencing at -6 degrees with no problem. But down here in East anglia even at +3 degrees it's so uncomfortable. 
I guess as water vapour is the major contributor of greenhouse warming, it makes sense that water vapour could also cause colder temperatures. Ot at least the feeling of colder temperatures.
I know that I get cold related sickness, such as colds and sore throats much more in damp wet cold than I ever did in the dry cold of the Highlands.

author: @DerekRoss1958
text:
There's a difference between feeling cold as a result of dry air or wet air outdoors at 0 degC dressed in cold weather gear, and feeling cold as a result of dry air or wet air indoors at a temperature of 20 degC. In my experience low humidity feels warmer than high humidity when fully dressed outdoors at low temperatures. However when more lightly dressed indoors at 20 degC, low humidity feels colder than high humidity.

In the summer when temperatures might be 25 degC or above, low humidity always feels colder than high humidity, indoors or out. So I think that the regulation of internal body heat has a lot to do with it. And in particular there's likely a change in how humidity affects your sense of cold when the external temperature rises to the point at which the body is trying to get rid of heat rather than to conserve it.

I hope my experience helps with context.

author: @jerrodlopes186
text: I'll add to the controversy by saying that certain US Navy SEALs and Tibetan Lamas would tell you that "cold" is all in the mind. Scientifically, of course, we can use thermometers to measure heat and the lack thereof. There's no doubt about that. How each individual experiences temperature is entirely subjective and is proven scientifically. The two groups mentioned above have been tested in full ice water submersion for twenty minutes with no appreciable drop in core temperature, whereas most of us would easily be hypothermic in the same conditions. Look to Wim Hoff as an excellent example. Moral of the story? You're only as cold as you decide to be.

author: @dorcusmallorcus6450
text: Is damp air not denser? Convection takes place in the air, but conduction happens at the skin. I can walk around in shirt  sleeves (for a while at least) on crisp, frosty nights (sadly rare now). Bracing.  But damp or wet nights are just miserable, moving or not.

I also kept my office on the chilly side all year round; in summer I would get "oh it's nice in here" but in winter (same office temperature) it was "why don't you have the heating on?"

What if we're talking about the humidity in a room which is being heated?

A bit like the wet bulb limit it may have to do with not losing as much heat through perspiration when the room is humid.

If this is the case I guess the question is whether it take more energy to heat a humid room to a low temperature Vs a dry room to a higher temperature.

Or again there could be no difference and cold is cold

author: @jonahbert111
text:
I don't know how these army experiments relate. being outdoors.  In my bedroom I have a propane portable radiant heater, and a small dehumidifier, and I would say dry is much better.  When I turn on the radiant heater @ about 59 / 60F, and run it ? 10 to 15 minutes, and at the same time I turn on the dehumidifier for 1 or 2 hours, with typical start humidity of 57%, the end result is around 48% humidity and about 64 to 65F, this is much better than if I do not run the dehumidifier and the humidity would be around 70+% at 65F.  At the same temperature, I feel much more comfy in the dry heat.  Use less propane.  

One gets a sizable amount of heat from the dehumidifier.  Consider how much heat is required to vaporize a pint of water on the stove.  The condensating of a pint of water puts out nearly as much heat.

author: @lilbullet158
text: For many years I used to be in the Plastering trade and for those who do not know this, there is a 'Heat Reflecting difference between bare plastered walls that have been painted and bare plastered walls that have been Wall papered (especially with painted lining paper). If you have 'Both' somewhere in your house go and put the flat of your hand on the Painted wall and then put your hand on the Papered wall. Your skin should be able to feel the slight difference in reflected heat. It may only be slight but that tiny bit of added insulation caused by the paper is certainly better than painted walls.

author: @VoidFame
text:
I think there are definitely many human factors such as disliking dreary days, disliking being wet etc., but one of the physical keys is probably the heat capacity of water. It may not matter as far as temperature goes, but for heat exchange it matters a lot. If you just went outside in the cold vs going outside and getting wet in the cold, it will take longer to get to a warm temperature if you're wet vs dry.

Imagine having to heat up only using a fireplace in those two scenarios. You'd even have the added horror of the evaporation of the water taking heat away from you.

replies.author: @jerrodlopes186
replies.text: Water saps body heat. If you ever capsize in a boat, the best bet to survive is to get out of the water and onto the keel of the boat. Though it will feel colder, normal people will retain body heat far longer in air vs. water.  Drying off ASAP is the better way to prevent hypothermia.

author: @brett4264
text: Living in Anchorage, I get to experience both very often. To me, I feel warmer when it's dry cold. It can be 0° and dry, and it feels warmer than a more humid cold 25° higher. (Temps in F)  But that's me.

author: @htmagic
text: RMS, air conditioning (AC) in the summer removes the humidity before it cools the air. So using a liquid dessicant and regenerating it can reduce the AC costs and make the system more efficient.

replies.author: @ShivaTD420
replies.text:
Removing the water from the air does cool the air. The water holds more heat, condensing it out of the air takes heat with it. 
Hot air holds more moisture, cooling the air down forces the water to fall out.

author: @corringhamdepot4434
text: If you want a real argument on social media just say "It's too cold to snow". 😉

author: @YOU_CANT_BE_THAT_STUPID
text: I'll say this, they dont say "Dont let your clothes get dry in the cold". Its when you are inactive that the blood vessels shrink, providing less warmth to the extremities. Wet acts as a hyper efficient heat sink when blood pressure drops.

author: @tutnallman
text: I have worked with Canadians in this country with the temperature around 0 degrees C. they were miserably cold, and I remember walking with them and listened to them moaning that their gear (clothing) was good for minus 20 C but in England, they were freezing. Chortle! Once the temperature stays below minus 3-4 everything gets drier and comfort is restored.......

replies.author: @wocookie2277
replies.text: Canadian here. Yes you’re sort of right about it getting dryer the colder it gets. Right now here in Manitoba we are at -36. It’s very dry out. But at this point wind plays a role. Windchills at -43. So warm is relative but below-10 better consider windchills.

author: @timteller1400
text: And all the bushcrafters and survivalists be like "You cold and wet, you soon be dead"

author: @The411
text: Scottish expat living in Canada. Wet cold is worse. We can have -40c at my front door, but it's extremely dry. Warm clothing and it's fine. I found wet cold can penetrate your layers right to the bone.

author: @jcor6689
text: first serious laugh I've had all day down here in the bunker

author: @shannongrant8591
text: 😂😂😂 That's so funny!!! I had the same image when you said that LMAO

author: @brett4264
text: Finally! "Thinking and Tinkering". TnT was bugging me.

author: @D-B-Cooper
text: I live in a rain forest, 165” a year, but it is a dry rain.

author: @yogiguitar1
text: wet cold is the worst! i grew up along the canal along the river lea in london and its the humidity that gets into your bones . now i live in france close to a river and its the same thing. its the humidity that gets you. but im used to it i suppose so there you go.

author: @notemachinist2314
text: Wet cold, once the cold gets to your bones it’s hard to get warm, you can wrap up against dry cold and stay warm 😁🙏🏼 Nameste, love your channel

replies.author: @deathstarHQ
replies.text: I live in Grimsby, a town with the word grim in it, we have patchy drizzle, put me ups and damp patches, it's never hot or cold really,  water just oozes into and out of everything, it even feels wetter than other water. the wind is referred to as guff's of wind, it can be completely still and a single Guff will find you occasionally carrying odor's it collected and forcefully rammed it in ya face.. and of course the wind is also wet.... I love Grimsby.... i bet you sounded miserable just reding it.. ha ha.
